title: Blink: CPU-Free LLM Inference by Delegating the Serving Stack to GPU and SmartNIC
summary:
Large Language Model (LLM) inference is rapidly becoming a core datacenter service, yet current serving stacks keep the host CPU on the critical path for orchestration and token-level control. This makes LLM performance sensitive to CPU interference, undermining application colocation and forcing operators to reserve CPU headroom, leaving substantial capacity unutilized.
  We introduce Blink, an end-to-end serving architecture that removes the host CPU from the steady-state inference path by redistributing responsibilities across a SmartNIC and a GPU. Blink offloads request handling to the SmartNIC, which delivers inputs directly into GPU memory via RDMA, and replaces host-driven scheduling with a persistent GPU kernel that performs batching, scheduling, and KV-cache management without CPU involvement.
  Evaluated against TensorRT-LLM, vLLM, and SGLang, Blink outperforms all baselines even in isolation, reducing pre-saturation P99 TTFT by up to 8.47$\times$ and P99 TPOT by up to 3.40$\times$, improving decode throughput by up to 2.1$\times$, and reducing energy per token by up to 48.6$\%$. Under CPU interference, Blink maintains stable performance, while existing systems degrade by up to two orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: Blink is a new LLM serving architecture that removes the CPU from the critical path by offloading orchestration to SmartNIC and GPU, improving performance and stability under CPU interference.
AI.motivation: Current LLM serving systems keep the host CPU on the critical path for orchestration, making performance sensitive to CPU interference and forcing operators to reserve CPU headroom, leaving substantial capacity unutilized.
AI.method: Blink redistributes responsibilities across a SmartNIC and GPU: offloads request handling to SmartNIC (delivers inputs via RDMA), replaces host-driven scheduling with persistent GPU kernel that performs batching, scheduling, and KV-cache management without CPU involvement.
AI.result: Outperforms TensorRT-LLM, vLLM, and SGLang: reduces P99 TTFT by up to 8.47×, P99 TPOT by up to 3.40×, improves decode throughput by up to 2.1×, reduces energy per token by up to 48.6%. Maintains stable performance under CPU interference while existing systems degrade by up to two orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Blink demonstrates that removing the CPU from the steady-state inference path enables more efficient, stable, and interference-resistant LLM serving, addressing critical datacenter deployment challenges.

title: Wattlytics: A Web Platform for Co-Optimizing Performance, Energy, and TCO in HPC Clusters
summary: The escalating computational demands and energy footprint of GPU-accelerated computing systems complicate informed design and operational decisions. We present the first release of Wattlytics (https://wattlytics.netlify.app), an interactive, browser-based decision-support system. Unlike existing procurement-oriented calculators, Wattlytics uniquely integrates benchmark-driven GPU performance scaling, dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS)-aware piecewise power modeling, and multi-year total cost of ownership (TCO) analysis within a single interactive environment. Users can configure heterogeneous systems across contemporary GPU architectures (GH200, H100, L40S, L40, A40, A100, and L4), select representative scientific workloads (e.g., GROMACS, AMBER), and explore deployment scenarios under constraints such as energy prices, system lifetime, and frequency scaling. Wattlytics computes multidimensional decision metrics (TCO breakdown, work-per-TCO, power-per-TCO, and work-per-watt-per-TCO) and supports design-space exploration, what-if scenarios, sensitivity metrics (elasticity, Sobol indices, Monte Carlo) and collaborative features to guide realistic cluster design and procurement under uncertainty. We demonstrate selected scenarios comparing deployment strategies under different operational modes: ixed budget, fixed GPU count, fixed performance, and fixed power. Our case studies show that, under budget or energy constraints, optimally deployed energy-efficient GPUs can outperform higher-performance alternatives in overall cost-effectiveness. Wattlytics helps users explore the design parameter space and distinguish between cost- and risk-driving factors, turning HPC design into a well-informed and explainable decision-making process.

AI.tldr: Wattlytics is an interactive browser-based decision-support system for GPU-accelerated computing that integrates performance scaling, power modeling, and TCO analysis to guide cluster design and procurement under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: The escalating computational demands and energy footprint of GPU-accelerated computing systems complicate informed design and operational decisions, creating a need for better decision-support tools.
AI.method: Wattlytics integrates benchmark-driven GPU performance scaling, DVFS-aware piecewise power modeling, and multi-year TCO analysis in an interactive browser environment. It supports heterogeneous system configuration across multiple GPU architectures, workload selection, and exploration of deployment scenarios under various constraints.
AI.result: The system computes multidimensional decision metrics (TCO breakdown, work-per-TCO, power-per-TCO, work-per-watt-per-TCO) and supports design-space exploration, what-if scenarios, sensitivity analysis, and collaborative features. Case studies show that optimally deployed energy-efficient GPUs can outperform higher-performance alternatives under budget or energy constraints.
AI.conclusion: Wattlytics helps users explore the design parameter space, distinguish between cost- and risk-driving factors, and turns HPC design into a well-informed and explainable decision-making process, demonstrating that energy-efficient GPU deployment can be more cost-effective than simply choosing the highest-performance alternatives.

title: FR3 for 6G Networks: A Comparative Study against FR1 and FR2 Across Diverse Environments
comment: This paper has been accepted for presentation at the 2026 EuCNC & 6G Summit. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2604.03992
summary: Motivated by increasing wireless capacity demands and 6G advancements, the newly defined Frequency Range 3 (FR3, 7.125-24.25 GHz), also known as the upper mid-band, has emerged as a promising spectrum candidate. It offers a balance between the large bandwidth potential of millimeter-wave bands and the favorable propagation characteristics of sub-6 GHz bands. As a result, the upper mid-band presents a strong opportunity to enhance both coverage and capacity, particularly for 6G systems and Cellular Vehicle-to-Base Station (C-V2B) communications. Harnessing this potential, however, requires addressing key technical challenges through accurate and realistic channel modeling across diverse urban environments, including Suburban, Urban, and HighRise Urban scenarios. To this end, we employ a ray-tracing tool to characterize downlink propagation and enable detailed channel modeling for reliable C-V2B links. We evaluate data rate performance across FR1 (sub-6 GHz), FR3, and FR2 (mmWave) bands using antenna array configurations designed for different urban environments. The results show that, under equal aperture sizes, FR3 achieves higher data rates than FR2 for cell-edge User Equipment (UEs) in both interference-free and full-interference scenarios, indicating that the additional array gain at mmWave is insufficient to fully compensate for the severe experienced path loss. Integrating one-hand-grip pedestrian UEs model into ray tracer shows that transitioning from vehicular to pedestrian UEs results in negligible differences in coverage probability (about 1\%--3\%) across all frequencies, with the minimum differences observed in FR3, particularly at 8.2 GHz.

AI.tldr: FR3 (7.125-24.25 GHz) offers balanced bandwidth and propagation for 6G/C-V2B, outperforming mmWave for cell-edge UEs despite equal aperture sizes, with minimal coverage difference between vehicular and pedestrian UEs.
AI.motivation: Address increasing wireless capacity demands and 6G advancements by exploring FR3 (upper mid-band) which offers a balance between large bandwidth potential of mmWave and favorable propagation characteristics of sub-6 GHz bands, particularly for enhancing coverage and capacity in 6G systems and C-V2B communications.
AI.method: Employ ray-tracing tool to characterize downlink propagation and enable detailed channel modeling for reliable C-V2B links across diverse urban environments (Suburban, Urban, HighRise Urban). Evaluate data rate performance across FR1 (sub-6 GHz), FR3, and FR2 (mmWave) bands using antenna array configurations designed for different urban environments, and integrate one-hand-grip pedestrian UEs model into ray tracer.
AI.result: Under equal aperture sizes, FR3 achieves higher data rates than FR2 for cell-edge UEs in both interference-free and full-interference scenarios, indicating that additional array gain at mmWave is insufficient to fully compensate for severe path loss. Transitioning from vehicular to pedestrian UEs results in negligible differences in coverage probability (about 1%-3%) across all frequencies, with minimum differences observed in FR3, particularly at 8.2 GHz.
AI.conclusion: FR3 presents a strong opportunity to enhance both coverage and capacity for 6G systems and C-V2B communications, outperforming mmWave bands for cell-edge users while maintaining minimal performance differences between vehicular and pedestrian UE types.

title: Position Paper: From Edge AI to Adaptive Edge AI
summary: Edge AI is often framed as model compression and deployment under tight constraints. We argue a stronger operational thesis: Edge AI in realistic deployments is necessarily adaptive. In long-horizon operation, a fixed (non-adaptive) configuration faces a fundamental failure mode: as data and operating conditions evolve and change in time, it must either (i) violate time-varying budgets (latency/energy/thermal/connectivity/privacy) or (ii) lose predictive reliability (accuracy and, critically, calibration), with risk concentrating in transient regimes and rare time intervals rather than in average performance. If a deployed system cannot reconfigure its computation - and, when required, its model state - under evolving conditions and constraints, it reduces to static embedded inference and cannot provide sustained utility. This position paper introduces a minimal Agent-System-Environment (ASE) lens that makes adaptivity precise at the edge by specifying (i) what changes, (ii) what is observed, (iii) what can be reconfigured, and (iv) which constraints must remain satisfied over time. Building on this framing, we formulate ten research challenges for the next decade, spanning theoretical guarantees for evolving systems, dynamic architectures and hybrid transitions between data-driven and model-based components, fault/anomaly-driven targeted updates, System-1/System-2 decompositions (anytime intelligence), modularity, validation under scarce labels, and evaluation protocols that quantify lifecycle efficiency and recovery/stability under drift and interventions.

AI.tldr: Edge AI must be adaptive to handle evolving data and constraints in real deployments, requiring reconfiguration of computation and model state over time rather than static inference.
AI.motivation: Current Edge AI approaches focus on model compression and deployment under fixed constraints, but fail in long-term operation as data and conditions evolve. Fixed configurations either violate time-varying budgets or lose predictive reliability, with risks concentrating in transient regimes.
AI.method: Proposes an Agent-System-Environment (ASE) framework to make adaptivity precise: specifying what changes, what is observed, what can be reconfigured, and which constraints must remain satisfied over time.
AI.result: Formulates ten research challenges for the next decade, including theoretical guarantees for evolving systems, dynamic architectures, fault-driven updates, System-1/System-2 decompositions, modularity, validation under scarce labels, and evaluation protocols for lifecycle efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Edge AI must embrace adaptivity as a core operational requirement, moving beyond static embedded inference to provide sustained utility through reconfigurable systems that maintain performance under evolving conditions and constraints.

title: Valve: Production Online-Offline Inference Colocation with Jointly-Bounded Preemption Latency and Rate
summary: LLM inference powers latency-critical production services nowadays. The bursty nature of inference traffic results in over-provisioning, which in turn leads to resource underutilization. While online-offline colocation promises to utilize idle capacity, broad production deployment must overcome two major challenges: (i) large online interference due to slow or frequent preemptions, and (ii) extensive frameworks and drivers modifications, to colocate different models and support preemptions. We present Valve, a production-friendly colocation system that jointly bounds preemption latency and preemption rate. Specifically, Valve enables sub-millisecond compute preemption at most once per online request, and rate-limited sub-layer memory reclamation. These guaranties are provided by a GPU runtime that combines channel-controlled compute isolation, page-fault-free memory reclamation, and dynamic memory reservation. Critically, Valve is practical to deploy, requiring one line of driver modification and 20 lines of framework patch. Deployed on 8,054 GPUs in production, Valve improves cluster utilization by 34.6%, which translates to a 2,170 GPU save. This efficiency gains is achieved with minimal online interference, incurring <5% TTFT increase and <2% TPOT increase across workloads.

AI.tldr: Valve is a production-friendly GPU colocation system that enables efficient online-offline workload sharing with minimal interference, achieving 34.6% utilization improvement with sub-millisecond compute preemptions.
AI.motivation: The bursty nature of LLM inference traffic leads to over-provisioning and resource underutilization in production services. While online-offline colocation could utilize idle capacity, production deployment faces challenges: large online interference from slow/frequent preemptions, and extensive framework/driver modifications needed for different model colocation and preemption support.
AI.method: Valve provides a GPU runtime with channel-controlled compute isolation, page-fault-free memory reclamation, and dynamic memory reservation. It enables sub-millisecond compute preemption at most once per online request, and rate-limited sub-layer memory reclamation. The system requires minimal deployment effort: one line of driver modification and 20 lines of framework patch.
AI.result: Deployed on 8,054 GPUs in production, Valve improves cluster utilization by 34.6%, translating to 2,170 GPU savings. It achieves this with minimal online interference: <5% TTFT (Time To First Token) increase and <2% TPOT (Time Per Output Token) increase across workloads.
AI.conclusion: Valve successfully addresses production colocation challenges by providing strong performance guarantees with practical deployment requirements, enabling significant resource savings while maintaining acceptable interference levels for latency-critical LLM inference services.

title: Energy-Efficient Drone Logistics for Last-Mile Delivery: Implications of Payload-Dependent Routing Strategies
summary: Drone delivery is rapidly emerging as a cost-effective and energy efficient alternative for last-mile delivery. Unlike ground vehicles, a drone's energy consumption depends on its payload in addition to travel distance. This creates a unique environmental challenge for multi-stop delivery tours, as the drone's total weight, and therefore its energy consumption rate, dynamically changes after each delivery. This paper investigates a novel green drone routing problem focused on maximizing energy efficiency. Through a series of motivating examples and numerical experiments, we demonstrate that energy-aware routing leads to several counter-intuitive routing strategies that contradict traditional distance-minimization delivery: a longer route may actually consume less energy than a shorter one; separate single-customer tours can be superior to a multi-stop tour; and a heterogeneous fleet, with drones of varying sizes, can achieve greater efficiency by matching drone capacity to specific delivery demands. In the numerical study, the green routing strategy shows energy savings in 67% of the instances. For these cases, the average energy saving is 2.17%, with a maximum saving of 5.97%, compared to minimum distance routing. These findings highlight the potential for green drone routing strategies to improve the sustainability of last-mile delivery.

AI.tldr: Green drone routing prioritizes energy efficiency over distance minimization, leading to counter-intuitive strategies like longer routes using less energy, separate single-customer tours, and heterogeneous fleets, achieving up to 5.97% energy savings.
AI.motivation: Drone delivery is emerging as a cost-effective last-mile solution, but unlike ground vehicles, drone energy consumption depends on both travel distance and payload weight, which dynamically changes during multi-stop deliveries, creating unique environmental challenges for routing optimization.
AI.method: The paper investigates a novel green drone routing problem through motivating examples and numerical experiments, comparing energy-aware routing strategies against traditional distance-minimization approaches for multi-stop delivery tours.
AI.result: Energy-aware routing shows energy savings in 67% of instances, with average savings of 2.17% and maximum savings of 5.97% compared to minimum distance routing. Counter-intuitive strategies include: longer routes consuming less energy, separate single-customer tours being superior to multi-stop tours, and heterogeneous fleets matching drone capacity to specific demands.
AI.conclusion: Green drone routing strategies have significant potential to improve the sustainability of last-mile delivery by prioritizing energy efficiency over traditional distance minimization, leading to substantial energy savings through counter-intuitive but effective routing approaches.

title: Self-Calibrating LLM-Based Analog Circuit Sizing with Interpretable Design Equations
comment: 11 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Artificial Intelligence (TCASAI)
summary: We present a self-calibrating framework for analog circuit sizing in which a large language model (LLM) derives topology-specific analytical design equations directly from a raw circuit netlist. Unlike existing AI-driven sizing methods where the model proposes parameter adjustments or reduces a search space, the LLM produces a complete Python sizing function tracing each device dimension to a specific performance constraint. A deterministic calibration loop extracts process-dependent parameters from a single transistor-level simulation, while a prediction-error feedback mechanism compensates for analytical inaccuracies. We validate the framework on six operational transconductance amplifier (OTA) topologies spanning three families at two process nodes (180 nm and 40 nm CMOS). All 12 topology-node combinations achieve all specifications, converging in 2-9 simulations for 11 of 12 cases, with one outlier requiring 16 simulations due to an extremely narrow feasible region. Despite large initial prediction errors, convergence depends on the measurement-feedback architecture, not prediction accuracy. This one-shot calibration automatically captures process-dependent variations, enabling cross-node portability without modification, retraining, or per-process characterization.

AI.tldr: LLM-based framework generates analytical design equations from circuit netlists, enabling automated analog circuit sizing with self-calibration across process nodes.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-driven sizing methods either propose parameter adjustments or reduce search spaces, lacking the ability to produce complete analytical sizing functions that can automatically adapt to different process nodes without retraining or per-process characterization.
AI.method: LLM derives topology-specific analytical design equations from raw circuit netlists, producing Python sizing functions. A deterministic calibration loop extracts process-dependent parameters from single transistor-level simulation, with prediction-error feedback mechanism compensating for analytical inaccuracies.
AI.result: Validated on 6 OTA topologies across 3 families at 180nm and 40nm CMOS nodes. All 12 topology-node combinations achieved specifications, converging in 2-9 simulations for 11 cases, with one outlier requiring 16 simulations due to extremely narrow feasible region.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables cross-node portability without modification, retraining, or per-process characterization. Convergence depends on measurement-feedback architecture rather than prediction accuracy, making it robust despite large initial prediction errors.

title: Reduced-Mass Orbital AI Inference via Integrated Solar, Compute, and Radiator Panels
summary: We describe and analyze a distributed compute architecture for SSO computational satellites that can potentially provide >100 kW compute power per launched metric ton (including deployment and station keeping mass). The architecture co-locates and integrates the solar cells, radiator, and compute functions into multiple small panels arranged in a large array. The resultant large vapor chamber radiator area per panel should permit ICs to operate at junction temperatures near 40*C with benefits in compute efficiency and reliability. Using the structure of the radiator to support the solar cells may also yield a specific power of about 500 W/kg compared to less than 100 for existing conventional implementations. Assuming development of custom solutions for all components, a 16 MW computation, 150 ton satellite comprising a 20 m x 2200 m grid of 16,000 panels can fit in a single Starship hold. The concept is scalable to much larger satellites with higher mass payloads or using on-orbit assembly. We consider panel sizes from 1 to 4 m2 to allow trading vapor chamber heat transport with compute efficiency and inter-panel communication. Assuming a 1 kW/panel design, 512-panel subarrays of the satellite can run a representative inference-only LLM with 500,000 token context window and 128 attention blocks, at a rate of 553 tokens/sec/session, across 256 simultaneous in-flight sessions. A full satellite could support 31 such subarrays, for >7900 inferences at a time.

AI.tldr: Distributed compute architecture for SSO satellites achieving >100 kW/ton compute power using integrated solar-radiator-compute panels, enabling large-scale AI inference in space.
AI.motivation: To create high-power computing satellites in space with superior specific power (500 W/kg vs <100 W/kg conventional) and efficient thermal management for reliable AI inference operations.
AI.method: Co-locate solar cells, radiators, and compute functions into small panels arranged in large arrays; use vapor chamber radiators for 40°C junction temperatures; scale from 1-4 m² panels; organize into 512-panel subarrays for LLM inference.
AI.result: 16 MW computation satellite (150 tons) fits in single Starship; 512-panel subarray runs LLM with 500k token context at 553 tokens/sec/session across 256 sessions; full satellite supports >7900 simultaneous inferences.
AI.conclusion: Distributed panel architecture enables scalable, high-power space computing with efficient thermal management, supporting large-scale AI inference applications in orbit.

title: Analytical Modeling of Dispersive Closed-loop MC Channels with Pulsatile Flow
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, Submitted for 13th ACM International Conference on Nanoscale Computing and Communication (NANOCOM27), St. Johns, Canada
summary: Molecular communication (MC) is a communication paradigm in which information is conveyed through the controlled release, propagation, and reception of molecules. Many envisioned healthcare applications of MC are expected to operate inside the human body. In this environment, the cardiovascular system ( CVS) acts as the physical channel, which forms a closed-loop network where particle transport is mainly governed by the combined effects of diffusion and flow. Despite the fact that physiological flows in many parts of the human body are inherently pulsatile due to the cardiac cycle, most existing models for dispersive closed-loop MC channels assume a constant flow velocity. In this paper, we present a time-variant one-dimensional (1D ) channel model for dispersive closed-loop MC systems with pulsatile flow. We derive an analytical expression for the channel impulse response (CIR ), which follows a wrapped Normal distribution with time-variant mean and variance. The obtained model reveals the cyclostationary nature of the channel and quantifies the influence of pulsation on the temporal concentration profile compared to steady-flow systems. Finally, the model is validated by three-dimensional ( 3D ) particle-based simulations (PBS s), showing excellent agreement and enabling an efficient analytical characterization of the channel.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a time-variant 1D channel model for molecular communication systems with pulsatile flow in closed-loop cardiovascular networks, deriving an analytical wrapped Normal distribution for the channel impulse response that captures cyclostationary behavior.
AI.motivation: Existing molecular communication models for dispersive closed-loop channels assume constant flow velocity, but physiological flows in the human body are inherently pulsatile due to the cardiac cycle. This gap needs to be addressed for accurate modeling of healthcare applications operating inside the human body.
AI.method: The authors present a time-variant one-dimensional channel model for dispersive closed-loop molecular communication systems with pulsatile flow. They derive an analytical expression for the channel impulse response that follows a wrapped Normal distribution with time-variant mean and variance, capturing the cyclostationary nature of the channel.
AI.result: The model reveals the cyclostationary nature of the channel and quantifies how pulsation affects the temporal concentration profile compared to steady-flow systems. The model is validated through 3D particle-based simulations, showing excellent agreement and enabling efficient analytical channel characterization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed time-variant model successfully captures pulsatile flow effects in closed-loop molecular communication channels, providing an accurate analytical framework that outperforms constant-flow assumptions and is validated by particle-based simulations.

title: SHIELD: A Segmented Hierarchical Memory Architecture for Energy-Efficient LLM Inference on Edge NPUs
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) inference on edge Neural Processing Units (NPUs) is fundamentally constrained by limited on-chip memory capacity. Although high-density embedded DRAM (eDRAM) is attractive for storing activation workspaces, its periodic refresh consumes substantial energy. Prior work has primarily focused on reducing off-chip traffic or optimizing refresh for persistent Key-Value (KV) caches, while transient and error-resilient Query and Attention Output (QO) activations are largely overlooked. We propose SHIELD, a lifecycle-aware segmented eDRAM architecture that jointly exploits temporal residency and bit-level sensitivity in bfloat16 (BF16) activations. SHIELD isolates the sign and exponent fields from the mantissa, disables refresh for transient QO mantissas, and applies relaxed refresh to persistent KV mantissas. Across multiple LLMs and inference scenarios, SHIELD reduces eDRAM refresh energy by 35% relative to a standard-refresh baseline while preserving accuracy on WikiText-2, PIQA, and ARC-Easy.

AI.tldr: SHIELD is a segmented eDRAM architecture that reduces refresh energy for LLM inference on NPUs by exploiting temporal residency and bit-level sensitivity of BF16 activations, achieving 35% energy savings while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLM inference on edge NPUs is constrained by limited on-chip memory, and while eDRAM is attractive for storing activation workspaces, its periodic refresh consumes substantial energy. Prior work focused on reducing off-chip traffic or optimizing refresh for persistent KV caches, but overlooked transient and error-resilient Query and Attention Output activations.
AI.method: SHIELD is a lifecycle-aware segmented eDRAM architecture that jointly exploits temporal residency and bit-level sensitivity in BF16 activations. It isolates sign and exponent fields from mantissa, disables refresh for transient QO mantissas, and applies relaxed refresh to persistent KV mantissas.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs and inference scenarios, SHIELD reduces eDRAM refresh energy by 35% relative to a standard-refresh baseline while preserving accuracy on WikiText-2, PIQA, and ARC-Easy benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SHIELD effectively addresses the energy overhead of eDRAM refresh for LLM inference by exploiting the unique characteristics of different activation types, achieving significant energy savings without compromising model accuracy.

title: Administrative Decentralization in Edge-Cloud Multi-Agent for Mobile Automation
summary: Collaborative edge-cloud frameworks have emerged as the main- stream paradigm for mobile automation, mitigating the latency and privacy risks inherent to monolithic cloud agents. However, existing approaches centralize administration in the cloud while relegating the device to passive execution, inducing a cognitive lag regard- ing real-time UI dynamics. To tackle this, we introduce AdecPilot by applying the principle of administrative decentralization to the edge-cloud multi-agent framework, which redefines edge agency by decoupling high-level strategic designing from tactical grounding. AdecPilot integrates a UI-agnostic cloud designer generating ab- stract milestones with a bimodal edge team capable of autonomous tactical planning and self-correction without cloud intervention. Furthermore, AdecPilot employs a Hierarchical Implicit Termi- nation protocol to enforce deterministic stops and prevent post- completion hallucinations. Extensive experiments demonstrate pro- posed approach improves task success rate by 21.7% while reducing cloud token consumption by 37.5% against EcoAgent and decreas- ing end to end latency by 88.9% against CORE. The source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Anonymous_code- B8AB.

AI.tldr: AdecPilot introduces administrative decentralization to edge-cloud mobile automation, separating high-level cloud strategy from autonomous edge tactical execution to reduce latency and improve task success.
AI.motivation: Existing edge-cloud frameworks centralize administration in the cloud while relegating devices to passive execution, creating cognitive lag regarding real-time UI dynamics and limiting responsiveness.
AI.method: AdecPilot applies administrative decentralization by decoupling high-level strategic designing from tactical grounding. It integrates a UI-agnostic cloud designer generating abstract milestones with a bimodal edge team capable of autonomous tactical planning and self-correction without cloud intervention, plus a Hierarchical Implicit Termination protocol to enforce deterministic stops.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show AdecPilot improves task success rate by 21.7%, reduces cloud token consumption by 37.5% against EcoAgent, and decreases end-to-end latency by 88.9% against CORE.
AI.conclusion: Administrative decentralization in edge-cloud frameworks effectively addresses cognitive lag and latency issues by empowering edge devices with autonomous tactical capabilities while maintaining cloud-level strategic oversight.

title: Trilinear Compute-in-Memory Architecture for Energy-Efficient Transformer Acceleration
summary: Self-attention in Transformers generates dynamic operands that force conventional Compute-in-Memory (CIM) accelerators into costly non-volatile memory (NVM) reprogramming cycles, degrading throughput and stressing device endurance. Existing solutions either reduce but retain NVM writes through matrix decomposition or sparsity, or move attention computation to digital CMOS at the expense of NVM density. We present TrilinearCIM, a Double-Gate FeFET (DG-FeFET)-based architecture that uses back-gate modulation to realize a three-operand multiply-accumulate primitive for in-memory attention computation without dynamic ferroelectric reprogramming. Evaluated on BERT-base (GLUE) and ViT-base (ImageNet and CIFAR), TrilinearCIM outperforms conventional CIM on seven of nine GLUE tasks while achieving up to 46.6\% energy reduction and 20.4\% latency improvement over conventional FeFET CIM at 37.3\% area overhead. To our knowledge, this is the first architecture to perform complete Transformer attention computation exclusively in NVM cores without runtime reprogramming.

AI.tldr: TrilinearCIM is a novel DG-FeFET-based CIM architecture that eliminates dynamic NVM reprogramming for Transformer attention computation, achieving better performance and energy efficiency than conventional CIM approaches.
AI.motivation: Self-attention in Transformers requires dynamic operands that force conventional CIM accelerators into costly NVM reprogramming cycles, degrading throughput and stressing device endurance. Existing solutions either retain NVM writes or move computation to digital CMOS, sacrificing density.
AI.method: TrilinearCIM uses Double-Gate FeFET (DG-FeFET) architecture with back-gate modulation to implement a three-operand multiply-accumulate primitive for in-memory attention computation without dynamic ferroelectric reprogramming.
AI.result: TrilinearCIM outperforms conventional CIM on 7 of 9 GLUE tasks with BERT-base, achieves up to 46.6% energy reduction and 20.4% latency improvement over conventional FeFET CIM at 37.3% area overhead, and maintains performance on ViT-base for ImageNet and CIFAR.
AI.conclusion: TrilinearCIM is the first architecture to perform complete Transformer attention computation exclusively in NVM cores without runtime reprogramming, addressing key limitations of conventional CIM accelerators for Transformer workloads.

title: FILCO: Flexible Composing Architecture with Real-Time Reconfigurability for DNN Acceleration
summary:
With the development of deep neural network (DNN) enabled applications, achieving high hardware resource efficiency on diverse workloads is non-trivial in heterogeneous computing platforms. Prior works discuss dedicated architectures to achieve maximal resource efficiency. However, a mismatch between hardware and workloads always exists in various diverse workloads. Other works discuss overlay architecture that can dynamically switch dataflow for different workloads. However, these works are still limited by flexibility granularity and induce much resource inefficiency.
  To solve this problem, we propose a flexible composing architecture, FILCO, that can efficiently match diverse workloads to achieve the optimal storage and computation resource efficiency. FILCO can be reconfigured in real-time and flexibly composed into a unified or multiple independent accelerators. We also propose the FILCO framework, including an analytical model with a two-stage DSE that can achieve the optimal design point. We also evaluate the FILCO framework on the 7nm AMD Versal VCK190 board. Compared with prior works, our design can achieve 1.3x - 5x throughput and hardware efficiency on various diverse workloads.

AI.tldr: FILCO is a flexible composing architecture that dynamically reconfigures to match diverse DNN workloads, achieving optimal storage and computation efficiency through real-time composition into unified or multiple independent accelerators.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches face limitations: dedicated architectures suffer from hardware-workload mismatches in diverse workloads, while overlay architectures have limited flexibility granularity and cause resource inefficiency. There's a need for a solution that can efficiently match diverse workloads with optimal resource utilization.
AI.method: Proposes FILCO architecture that can be reconfigured in real-time and flexibly composed into unified or multiple independent accelerators. Also introduces FILCO framework with analytical model and two-stage Design Space Exploration (DSE) to achieve optimal design points.
AI.result: Evaluation on 7nm AMD Versal VCK190 board shows FILCO achieves 1.3x - 5x throughput and hardware efficiency improvements compared to prior works across various diverse workloads.
AI.conclusion: FILCO successfully addresses the hardware-workload mismatch problem in heterogeneous computing platforms by providing flexible composition capabilities that enable optimal resource efficiency for diverse DNN workloads.

title: LogAct: Enabling Agentic Reliability via Shared Logs
summary: Agents are LLM-driven components that can mutate environments in powerful, arbitrary ways. Extracting guarantees for the execution of agents in production environments can be challenging due to asynchrony and failures. In this paper, we propose a new abstraction called LogAct, where each agent is a deconstructed state machine playing a shared log. In LogAct, agentic actions are visible in the shared log before they are executed; can be stopped prior to execution by pluggable, decoupled voters; and recovered consistently in the case of agent or environment failure. LogAct enables agentic introspection, allowing the agent to analyze its own execution history using LLM inference, which in turn enables semantic variants of recovery, health check, and optimization. In our evaluation, LogAct agents recover efficiently and correctly from failures; debug their own performance; optimize token usage in swarms; and stop all unwanted actions for a target model on a representative benchmark with just a 3% drop in benign utility.

AI.tldr: LogAct is a new abstraction for LLM-driven agents that treats each agent as a deconstructed state machine playing a shared log, enabling pre-execution action visibility, pluggable voting for action stopping, and consistent recovery from failures.
AI.motivation: Extracting guarantees for agent execution in production environments is challenging due to asynchrony and failures. Current agent systems lack mechanisms for safe execution, recovery, and introspection.
AI.method: LogAct deconstructs agents into state machines that interact with a shared log. Agentic actions are first written to the log before execution, allowing pluggable voters to stop unwanted actions. The system enables agentic introspection where agents can analyze their own execution history using LLM inference.
AI.result: LogAct agents recover efficiently and correctly from failures, debug their own performance, optimize token usage in swarms, and stop all unwanted actions for a target model with only a 3% drop in benign utility on a representative benchmark.
AI.conclusion: LogAct provides a practical abstraction for safe agent execution with built-in recovery, introspection, and control mechanisms, addressing key challenges in deploying LLM-driven agents in production environments.

title: From LLM to Silicon: RL-Driven ASIC Architecture Exploration for On-Device AI Inference
summary: We present an RL-driven compiler that jointly optimizes ASIC architecture, memory hierarchy, and workload partitioning for AI inference across 3nm to 28nm. The design space is formulated as a single Markov Decision Process with mixed discrete-continuous actions and a unified Power-Performance-Area (PPA) objective. Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) with Mixture-of-Experts gating explores the joint space of mesh topology, per-core microarchitecture, and operator placement. We validate on two workloads, Llama 3.1 8B FP16 (high-performance mode, 29809 tokens per second at 3nm) and SmolVLM (low-power mode, less than 13 mW at all nodes, 10 MHz). Across 7 process nodes, the RL automatically adapts mesh sizes and per-tile configurations, including heterogeneous FETCH, VLEN, and memory allocation without node-specific manual retuning.

AI.tldr: An RL-driven compiler jointly optimizes ASIC architecture, memory hierarchy, and workload partitioning for AI inference across multiple process nodes (3nm to 28nm) using a unified PPA objective.
AI.motivation: To automate the complex joint optimization of ASIC architecture, memory hierarchy, and workload partitioning for AI inference across multiple process nodes without manual retuning for each node.
AI.method: Formulate the design space as a single Markov Decision Process with mixed discrete-continuous actions, using Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) with Mixture-of-Experts gating to explore joint space of mesh topology, per-core microarchitecture, and operator placement.
AI.result: Achieved 29809 tokens per second for Llama 3.1 8B FP16 at 3nm (high-performance mode) and less than 13 mW for SmolVLM across all nodes at 10 MHz (low-power mode). RL automatically adapts configurations across 7 process nodes without manual retuning.
AI.conclusion: The RL-driven compiler successfully automates joint optimization of ASIC design for AI inference across multiple process nodes, demonstrating effective adaptation to different performance-power tradeoffs and process technologies.

title: LegoDiffusion: Micro-Serving Text-to-Image Diffusion Workflows
summary: Text-to-image generation executes a diffusion workflow comprising multiple models centered on a base diffusion model. Existing serving systems treat each workflow as an opaque monolith, provisioning, placing, and scaling all constituent models together, which obscures internal dataflow, prevents model sharing, and enforces coarse-grained resource management. In this paper, we make a case for micro-serving diffusion workflows with LegoDiffusion, a system that decomposes a workflow into loosely coupled model-execution nodes that can be independently managed and scheduled. By explicitly managing individual model inference, LegoDiffusion unlocks cluster-scale optimizations, including per-model scaling, model sharing, and adaptive model parallelism. Collectively, LegoDiffusion outperforms existing diffusion workflow serving systems, sustaining up to 3x higher request rates and tolerating up to 8x higher burst traffic.

AI.tldr: LegoDiffusion is a system that decomposes text-to-image diffusion workflows into independent model-execution nodes for fine-grained resource management and optimization, achieving 3x higher request rates and 8x better burst tolerance.
AI.motivation: Existing serving systems treat diffusion workflows as opaque monoliths, which obscures internal dataflow, prevents model sharing, and enforces coarse-grained resource management, limiting efficiency and scalability.
AI.method: LegoDiffusion decomposes diffusion workflows into loosely coupled model-execution nodes that can be independently managed and scheduled, enabling per-model scaling, model sharing, and adaptive model parallelism.
AI.result: LegoDiffusion outperforms existing diffusion workflow serving systems, sustaining up to 3x higher request rates and tolerating up to 8x higher burst traffic through cluster-scale optimizations.
AI.conclusion: Micro-serving diffusion workflows through decomposition into independent model-execution nodes enables significant performance improvements and better resource utilization compared to monolithic serving approaches.

title: Scheduling Coflows in Multi-Core OCS Networks with Performance Guarantee
summary:
Coflow provides a key application-layer abstraction for capturing communication patterns, enabling the efficient coordination of parallel data flows to reduce job completion times in distributed systems. Modern data center networks (DCNs) are employing multiple independent optical circuit switching (OCS) cores operating concurrently to meet the massive bandwidth demands of application jobs. However, existing coflow scheduling research primarily focuses on the single-core setting, with multi-core fabrics only for EPS (electrical packet switching) networks.
  To address this gap, this paper studies the coflow scheduling problem in multi-core OCS networks under the not-all-stop reconfiguration model in which one circuit's reconfiguration does not interrupt other circuits. The challenges stem from two aspects: (i) cross-core coupling induced by traffic assignment across heterogeneous cores; and (ii) per-core OCS scheduling constraints, namely port exclusivity and reconfiguration delay. We propose an approximation algorithm that jointly integrates cross-core flow assignment and per-core circuit scheduling to minimize the total weighted coflow completion time (CCT) and establish a provable worst-case performance guarantee. Furthermore, our algorithm framework can be directly applied to the multi-core EPS scenario with the corresponding approximation ratio under packet-switched fabrics. Trace-driven simulations using real Facebook workloads demonstrate that our algorithm effectively reduces weighted CCT and tail CCT.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses coflow scheduling in multi-core optical circuit switching networks, proposing an approximation algorithm that jointly handles cross-core flow assignment and per-core circuit scheduling to minimize weighted coflow completion time.
AI.motivation: Modern data centers use multiple optical circuit switching cores for high bandwidth, but existing coflow scheduling research focuses on single-core settings. There's a gap in handling multi-core OCS networks with cross-core coupling and per-core scheduling constraints.
AI.method: Proposed an approximation algorithm that jointly integrates cross-core flow assignment and per-core circuit scheduling under the not-all-stop reconfiguration model. The algorithm handles port exclusivity and reconfiguration delay constraints while minimizing total weighted coflow completion time.
AI.result: The algorithm provides provable worst-case performance guarantees. Trace-driven simulations using real Facebook workloads show effective reduction in both weighted CCT and tail CCT. The framework also applies to multi-core EPS networks with corresponding approximation ratios.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the coflow scheduling problem in multi-core OCS networks, providing a practical solution with theoretical guarantees that improves job completion times in modern data center networks.

title: A Full-Stack Performance Evaluation Infrastructure for 3D-DRAM-based LLM Accelerators
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) exhibit memory-intensive behavior during decoding, making it a key bottleneck in LLM inference. To accelerate decoding execution, hybrid-bonding-based 3D-DRAM has been adopted in LLM accelerators. While this emerging technology provides strong performance gains over existing hardware, current 3D-DRAM accelerators (3D-Accelerators) rely on closed-source evaluation tools, limiting access to publicly available performance analysis methods. Moreover, existing designs are highly customized for specific scenarios, lacking a general and reusable full-stack modeling for 3D-Accelerators across diverse usecases.
  To bridge this fundamental gap, we present ATLAS, the first silicon-proven Architectural Three-dimesional-DRAM-based LLM Accelerator Simulation framework. Built on commercially deployed multi-layer 3D-DRAM technology, ATLAS introduces unified abstractions for both 3D-Accelerator system architecture and programming primitives to support arbitrary LLM inference scenarios. Validation against real silicon shows that ATLAS achieves $\le$8.57% simulation error and 97.26-99.96\% correlation with measured performance. Through design space exploration with ATLAS, we demonstrate its ability to guide architecture design and distill key takeaways for both 3D-DRAM memory system and 3D-Accelerator microarchitecture across scenarios. ATLAS will be open-sourced upon publication, enabling further research on 3D-Accelerators.

AI.tldr: ATLAS is the first silicon-proven simulation framework for 3D-DRAM-based LLM accelerators, addressing the memory bottleneck in LLM inference with accurate modeling and design space exploration capabilities.
AI.motivation: LLMs have memory-intensive decoding that bottlenecks inference. While hybrid-bonding 3D-DRAM accelerators offer performance gains, current tools are closed-source and scenario-specific, lacking general modeling frameworks for diverse use cases.
AI.method: ATLAS introduces unified abstractions for 3D-Accelerator system architecture and programming primitives, built on commercially deployed multi-layer 3D-DRAM technology to support arbitrary LLM inference scenarios.
AI.result: Validation against real silicon shows ≤8.57% simulation error and 97.26-99.96% correlation with measured performance. ATLAS enables design space exploration and provides key insights for 3D-DRAM memory systems and accelerator microarchitecture.
AI.conclusion: ATLAS bridges the gap in 3D-Accelerator modeling, offering an accurate, open-source simulation framework that will enable further research and guide architecture design for memory-efficient LLM inference acceleration.

title: Asynchronous Quantum Distributed Computing: Causality, Snapshots, and Global Operations
summary: We initiate the study of asynchronous quantum distributed systems, focusing on the case of implementing atomic quantum global operations that can be decomposed into a collection of local operations on the components of the system. A simple example of such an operation is a quantum snapshot in which the whole system is instantaneously measured. Based on the classical snapshot algorithm of Chandy and Lamport, we design a quantum distributed algorithm to implement such decomposable global operations, which we call the QGO Algorithm. The analysis of our algorithm shows that arguments based on Lamport's computational causality remain valid in the quantum world, even though, due to entanglement, causality is not manifest from the standard description of the system in terms of a (global) quantum state. Our other contributions include a formal model of quantum distributed computing, and a formal specification for the desired behavior of a global operation, which may be of interest even in classical settings (such as in the setting of randomized algorithms).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces quantum distributed systems, proposes an algorithm for implementing atomic quantum global operations (like quantum snapshots), and shows that classical causality arguments remain valid in quantum settings despite entanglement challenges.
AI.motivation: To study asynchronous quantum distributed systems and implement atomic quantum global operations that can be decomposed into local operations, addressing the challenge of maintaining causality in quantum systems where entanglement complicates traditional state descriptions.
AI.method: Developed the QGO Algorithm based on Chandy and Lamport's classical snapshot algorithm, designed to implement decomposable global operations in quantum distributed systems, with formal models and specifications for quantum distributed computing.
AI.result: Demonstrated that Lamport's computational causality arguments remain valid in quantum systems despite entanglement, and provided formal models and specifications that are applicable even in classical randomized algorithm settings.
AI.conclusion: Successfully initiated the study of asynchronous quantum distributed systems, showing that classical distributed computing concepts can be extended to quantum settings while maintaining causality principles, with potential applications beyond quantum computing.

title: City-Scale Visibility Graph Analysis via GPU-Accelerated HyperBall
summary: Visibility Graph Analysis (VGA) is a key space syntax method for understanding how spatial configuration shapes human movement, but its reliance on all-pairs BFS computation limits practical application to small study areas. We present a system that combines three techniques to scale VGA to city-scale problems: (i) delta-compressed CSR storage using LEB128 varint encoding, which achieves ~4x compression and enables memory-mapped graphs exceeding available RAM; (ii) HyperBall, a probabilistic distance estimator based on HyperLogLog counter propagation, applied here for the first time to visibility graphs, reducing BFS complexity from O(N|E|) to O(D|E|2^p); and (iii) GPU-accelerated CUDA kernels with a fused decode-union kernel that streams the compressed graph via PCIe and performs LEB128 decoding entirely in shared memory. HyperBall's iteration count equals the topological depth limit, so the radius-n analysis that practitioners already use as standard translates directly into proportional speedup -- unlike depthmapX, whose BFS time is invariant to depth setting due to the small diameter of visibility graphs. Using depthmapX's own visibility algorithm (sparkSieve2) to ensure identical edge sets, our tool achieves a 239x end-to-end speedup at 42,705 cells and scales to 236,000 cells (4.8 billion edges) in 137 seconds -- problem sizes far beyond depthmapX's practical limit. At p=10, Visual Mean Depth achieves Pearson r=0.999 with 1.7% median relative error across 20 matched configurations.

AI.tldr: A system combining compressed graph storage, HyperLogLog-based probabilistic distance estimation, and GPU acceleration enables city-scale visibility graph analysis with 239x speedup over existing tools.
AI.motivation: Visibility Graph Analysis (VGA) is limited to small study areas due to computational complexity of all-pairs BFS, preventing practical city-scale applications.
AI.method: Three techniques: (1) delta-compressed CSR storage with LEB128 encoding for memory efficiency; (2) HyperBall probabilistic distance estimator using HyperLogLog counters; (3) GPU-accelerated CUDA kernels with fused decode-union operations.
AI.result: 239x end-to-end speedup at 42,705 cells, scales to 236,000 cells (4.8B edges) in 137 seconds, achieves Pearson r=0.999 with 1.7% median relative error for Visual Mean Depth.
AI.conclusion: The system successfully scales VGA to city-scale problems by overcoming computational bottlenecks through compression, probabilistic estimation, and GPU acceleration.

title: NL-CPS: Reinforcement Learning-Based Kubernetes Control Plane Placement in Multi-Region Clusters
comment: Accepted to be published in the 10th IEEE International Conference on Fog and Edge Computing
summary: The placement of Kubernetes control-plane nodes is critical to ensuring cluster reliability, scalability, and performance, and therefore represents a significant deployment challenge in heterogeneous, multi-region environments. Existing initialisation procedures typically select control-plane hosts arbitrarily, without considering node resource capacity or network topology, often leading to suboptimal cluster performance and reduced resilience. Given Kubernetes's status as the de facto standard for container orchestration, there is a need to rigorously evaluate how control-plane node placement influences the overall performance of the cluster operating across multiple regions. This paper advances this goal by introducing an intelligent methodology for selecting control-plane node placement across dynamically selected Cloud-Edge resources spanning multiple regions, as part of an automated orchestration system. More specifically, we propose a reinforcement learning framework based on neural contextual bandits that observes operational performance and learns optimal control-plane placement policies from infrastructure characteristics. Experimental evaluation across several geographically distributed regions and multiple cluster configurations demonstrates substantial performance improvements over several baseline approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a reinforcement learning framework using neural contextual bandits to intelligently select Kubernetes control-plane node placement across multi-region Cloud-Edge environments, achieving significant performance improvements over baseline approaches.
AI.motivation: Current Kubernetes control-plane node placement methods are arbitrary and don't consider node resource capacity or network topology, leading to suboptimal performance and reduced resilience in heterogeneous, multi-region environments. As Kubernetes is the de facto standard for container orchestration, there's a need to rigorously evaluate how control-plane placement influences overall cluster performance.
AI.method: The paper proposes a reinforcement learning framework based on neural contextual bandits that observes operational performance and learns optimal control-plane placement policies from infrastructure characteristics. This intelligent methodology selects control-plane node placement across dynamically selected Cloud-Edge resources spanning multiple regions as part of an automated orchestration system.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation across several geographically distributed regions and multiple cluster configurations demonstrates substantial performance improvements over several baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed intelligent control-plane placement methodology using reinforcement learning significantly improves Kubernetes cluster performance and resilience in multi-region Cloud-Edge environments compared to arbitrary placement approaches.

title: Taming GPU Underutilization via Static Partitioning and Fine-grained CPU Offloading
comment: To be published in Proceedings of ISC High Performance 2026
summary: Advances in GPU compute throughput and memory capacity brings significant opportunities to a wide range of workloads. However, efficiently utilizing these resources remains challenging, particularly because diverse application characteristics may result in imbalanced utilization. Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) is a promising approach to improve utilization by partitioning GPU compute and memory resources into fixed-size slices with isolation. Yet, its effectiveness and limitations in supporting HPC workloads remain an open question. We present a comprehensive system-level characterization of different GPU sharing options using real-world scientific, AI, and data analytics applications, including NekRS, LAMMPS, Llama3, and Qiskit. Our analysis reveals that while GPU sharing via MIG can significantly reduce resource underutilization, and enable system-level improvements in throughput and energy, interference still occurs through shared resources, such as power throttling. Our performance-resource scaling results indicate that coarse-grained provisioning for tightly coupled compute and memory resources often mismatches application needs. To address this mismatch, we propose a memory-offloading scheme that leverages the cache-coherent Nvlink-C2C interconnect to bridge the gap between coarse-grained resource slices and reduce resource underutilization.

AI.tldr: MIG partitioning improves GPU utilization but has limitations for HPC workloads due to interference and coarse-grained resource allocation mismatches; memory-offloading via Nvlink-C2C is proposed to address these issues.
AI.motivation: GPU resources are underutilized due to diverse application characteristics causing imbalanced utilization. MIG partitioning offers isolation but its effectiveness for HPC workloads remains uncertain.
AI.method: Comprehensive system-level characterization of GPU sharing options using real-world applications (NekRS, LAMMPS, Llama3, Qiskit). Performance-resource scaling analysis and proposal of memory-offloading scheme using Nvlink-C2C interconnect.
AI.result: MIG reduces resource underutilization and improves system throughput and energy efficiency, but interference occurs through shared resources like power throttling. Coarse-grained provisioning often mismatches application needs.
AI.conclusion: Memory-offloading via Nvlink-C2C can bridge the gap between coarse-grained resource slices and reduce underutilization, addressing MIG limitations for HPC workloads.
